text: We present you a laparoscopic right amicolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis in a medial to lateral approach in a standardized way, allowing the easy learning of the trainee. Our patient was a sixty-six year old female previously streptomized by infraumbilical median laparotomy with a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma of the cecum proposed for a laparoscopic right amicolectomy. In the preoperative CT scan is visible the neoplastic lesion in the sacral pole and the absence of metastatic lesions. We placed four trochars, two of ten millimeters, one in the umbilicus for the laparoscopic otic and another suprapubic for the left end of the surgeon, and two of five millimeters, one in the left iliac fossa for the right end of the surgeon and another in the upper left quadrant for the assistant. Both the surgeon and the assistant were on the left side of the patient. We outline the surgical procedure in eight fundamental steps that will be explained one by one throughout this video.

The surgeon confirms the location of the lesion and inspects the abdominal cavity. The ileocecal region is exposed, and traction on the mesentery reveals the ileocolic vessels. Dissection begins with a small opening in the mesentery below these vessels. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg and left tilt for optimal exposure, with the greater omentum mobilized towards the liver.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels and applies two hemostatic clips before sectioning them with a bipolar electrosurgery device. The creation of the submesocolic space begins, using blunt traction and counter traction to facilitate dissection. The third portion of the duodenum serves as a reference point for the dissection plane, which progresses cranially towards the liver. A gauze is placed in the submesocolic space for future reference.

The surgeon opens the visceral sheath of the mesocolic peritoneum and proceeds with the sectioning of the mesocolon. The right middle colic branch is identified, and two hemostatic clips are applied before sectioning the vessel with a bipolar electrosurgery device.

The surgeon continues the mesocolon section until reaching the transverse colon. The greater omentum is sectioned, opening the lesser sac and releasing the hepatic angle, facilitated by the pneumoperitoneum and previous dissection of the submesocolic space. Dissection proceeds along the right peritoneal gutter, releasing lateral adhesions to the Toldt's fascia. The ileocecal region is immobilized as part of the fifth step.

The surgeon uses a linear mechanical stapler device to section the mesentery and terminal ileum, followed by the transverse colon. The right colon is released, and intracorporeal anastomosis is performed, visually aligning the two cut ends of the bowel within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic lateral-to-lateral anastomosis using a linear mechanical stapler with a sixty millimeter vascular load. The intestinal orifice is then closed with a non-resorbable continuous suture.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a four-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision using an endo bag. A greater omentum is placed in the mesenteric defect to reduce the risk of internal hernias.

Pathology report revealed the PT-IV-A N-II-B with lymphovascular permeation and metastasis in thirteen of thirty-two excised lymph nodes. The patient underwent adjuvant chemotherapy. This video proves that, despite a greater technical complexity, it is possible for trainees to perform this procedure safely and with good oncological results if well supervised and with a standardized approach.
